chemicals.name: The primary toxic substance in cherries
Amygdalin (and its toxic metabolite, Hydrogen Cyanide)

chemicals.concentration_notes: or potency: Amygdalin naturally accumulates in high concentrations within the pits (kernels) of the fruit. Specifically, red cherry kernels contain about 3.9 g/kg of amygdalin, while black cherry kernels contain roughly 2.7 g/kg7. The hydrogen cyanide released from this compound is extremely potent; the fatal oral dose of cyanide is generally estimated to be between 0.6 and 1.5 mg/kg of body weigh
chemicals.description: in cats: Amygdalin is not directly toxic on its own; however, when a cat chews or crushes the cherry pit, stem, or leaves, the compound mixes with β-glucosidase enzymes (present in the plant tissue or the cat's own intestinal microbiota)24. These enzymes hydrolyze the amygdalin, ultimately decomposing it to instantly release hydrogen cyanide (HCN)25. Cyanide is a potent and lethal toxin that binds
chemicals.name: Hydrogen Cyanide is HCN

description: Cherries present a split safety profile for cats. The ripe, pitted fruit flesh is not toxic and is safe in very small amounts. The danger lies in the pits, stems, leaves, and bark, which all contain amygdalin — a cyanogenic glycoside that digestive enzymes convert into hydrogen cyanide (HCN). Cyanide blocks cytochrome c oxidase, the enzyme that enables cells to use oxygen for energy, causing rapid cellular asphyxiation. The heart and brain, with their high oxygen demands, are the first to fail.
emergencyNote: The seeds (pits), stems, and leaves of cherries contain amygdalin
name: Cherries
safetyNotes: Symptoms (if pits or leaves ingested): Vomiting, rapid or laboured breathing, lethargy, low blood pressure, shock, seizures, and potentially fatal cardiac and respiratory failure.
Safe part: Pitted ripe flesh only — fully remove pit, stem, and any leaves before offering a small piece as a treat.
Physical hazard: Pits pose a choking and intestinal obstruction risk even before releasing cyanide.
Action: Emergency veterinary care if pit, bark, or leaves have been chewed.

symptoms.name: Difficulty Breathing and Hyperventilation
symptoms.notes: The hydrogen cyanide released from crushed cherry pits, stems, or leaves acts directly to inhibit cellular oxygen metabolism1. This forces the cat to hyperventilate and gasp for air as its body attempts to compensate for the sudden inability of its cells to extract and utilize oxygen
symptoms.onset: Rapid onset (often within minutes to a few hours).

symptoms.name: Vomiting and Diarrhea
symptoms.notes: This can occur as an initial reaction to the cyanide toxicity or due to the physical irritation of the plant material and seeds passing through the digestive tract
symptoms.onset: Shortly after ingestion (often within 30 minutes to a few hours).

symptoms.name: Convulsions, Seizures, and Twitching
symptoms.notes: Because the brain is a high-oxygen-demand organ, the sudden halt in ATP (energy) production caused by cyanide poisoning quickly deprives the brain of energy, leading to profound neurological distress and involuntary motor dysfunction

symptoms.name: Coma and Shock
symptoms.notes: As the cyanide completely halts cellular respiration in vital organs, the resulting rapid cellular death in the brain and heart leads to cardiovascular collapse, loss of consciousness, coma, and ultimately death if untreated
symptoms.onset: Rapidly following respiratory distress and seizures.

symptoms.name: Cherry-Red Mucous Membranes
symptoms.notes: Because cyanide blocks the cells from taking up oxygen from the bloodstream, venous blood remains fully saturated with oxygen. This causes a distinct, abnormally bright red coloration of the cat's skin, gums, and mucous membranes

symptoms.name: Gastrointestinal Obstruction (Physical Hazard)
symptoms.notes: Aside from chemical toxicity, swallowing an intact, hard cherry pit presents a major physical hazard. The undigested pit can become lodged in the cat's digestive tract, causing a dangerous mechanical obstruction
symptoms.onset: Variable (can take hours to days for symptoms like persistent vomiting and severe abdominal pain to

treatments.name: The seeds (pits), stems, and leaves of cherries contain amygdalin

treatments.description: of the procedure: Placing the cat in an oxygen cage or providing flow-by oxygen to maximize the amount of oxygen available in the bloodstream. Any situation-specific
treatments.name: Oxygen Therapy (Life-Saving Stabilization)
treatments.notes: This is the absolute highest priority for a symptomatic cat. It is strictly required for cats suffering from cyanide poisoning because the toxin compromises the cells' ability to extract and utilize oxygen, rapidly suffocating high-oxygen-demand organs like the brain and heart

treatments.description: of the procedure: A veterinarian administers an α 2 ​ -adrenergic agonist, such as dexmedetomidine, to safely force the cat to vomit and expel the cherry parts before the plant material can release more cyanide into the system4. Any situation-specific
treatments.name: Induction of Emesis (Early Gastrointestinal Decontamination)
treatments.notes: This intervention is the primary goal if the ingestion is caught very early (within 1 to 2 hours) and the cat is still asymptomatic4. Pet owners must never attempt to use 3% hydrogen peroxide to induce vomiting at home, as it is strictly contraindicated for cats and can cause fatal hemorrhagic gastr

treatments.description: of the procedure: Administering oral activated charcoal to bind to and adsorb any residual toxins remaining in the stomach and gastrointestinal tract4. Any situation-specific
treatments.name: Administration of Activated Charcoal (Secondary Decontamination)
treatments.notes: This is typically administered immediately following successful emesis to trap any remaining cyanogenic compounds in the gut and prevent them from being absorbed into the bloodstream

treatments.description: of the procedure: Hospitalizing the cat to administer continuous IV fluids and other medications to support failing organ systems3. Any situation-specific
treatments.name: Intravenous (IV) Fluid Therapy and Supportive Care
treatments.notes: Because cyanide poisoning strikes the cardiovascular and central nervous systems so violently, aggressive fluid therapy is needed to maintain blood pressure, ensure tissue perfusion, and support the cat through systemic shock

treatments.description: of the procedure: Utilizing an endoscope or performing abdominal surgery to physically extract intact cherry pits from the digestive tract. Any situation-specific
treatments.name: Mechanical Extraction (Obstruction Management)
treatments.notes: While crushed cherry pits release deadly cyanide, swallowing a hard, intact fruit pit presents a different but equally dangerous physical hazard25. Like the seeds of other stone fruits, whole cherry pits can easily become lodged in the throat or intestinal tract, causing a dangerous mechanical obstr
